taskDescription: Click on the key anatomical terms related to the back of the leg and sole in the text below.
textField:
The superficial posterior compartment of the leg contains *gastrocnemius* and *soleus*, which together form the *triceps* *surae*. Their tendons merge to form the *calcaneal* (*Achilles*) tendon, the thickest and strongest tendon in the body. The *avascular* zone, located 2-6 cm above the calcaneal insertion, is the most common site of rupture. The *Thompson* test is used clinically to diagnose calcaneal tendon rupture by squeezing the calf.

The deep posterior compartment contains *tibialis* *posterior*, *flexor* *digitorum* *longus*, and *flexor* *hallucis* *longus*. These tendons pass behind the *medial* *malleolus* in order remembered by the mnemonic 'Tom, Dick, ANd Very Nervous Harry'. The *tibial* nerve and *posterior* *tibial* artery also pass behind the medial malleolus.

*Plantar* *fasciitis* is the most common cause of heel pain. The sole is supplied by the *medial* and *lateral* *plantar* nerves, both terminal branches of the tibial nerve. The *medial* plantar nerve is the equivalent of the median nerve in the hand.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the posterior leg and sole anatomy and try again.
Good effort! Review the terms you missed.
Excellent! You identified the key anatomical terms accurately.
